extra.reasoning:
slurred_speech appears in exactly one rule: interaction_drop (priority 100), the highest-priority rule, which sets acuity := self_care (the LOWEST acuity) when chest_pain AND palpitations AND hemoptysis AND rash AND slurred_speech are all true. The monotonicity property requires that flipping slurred_speech from false to true (other inputs fixed) never lowers acuity.

Counterexample compares two inputs identical except slurred_speech, with chest_pain=palpitations=hemoptysis=rash=true, dyspnea=false:

BEFORE (slurred_speech=false): interaction_drop does not fire. Matching rules: raise_chest_pain(urgent,p2), raise_hemoptysis(urgent,p2), raise_rash(emergent,p3), raise_palpitations(emergent,p3). Highest priority among matches is p3 -> acuity = emergent (highest enum value).

AFTER (slurred_speech=true): interaction_drop now fires (all five conjuncts true) at priority 100, beating every other rule -> acuity := self_care (lowest enum value). The other rules (p2/p3) are dominated by p100.

Enum order low-to-high is [self_care, routine, urgent, emergent], so acuity drops from emergent (highest) to self_care (lowest) when slurred_speech is set true. This lowers acuity, directly violating the monotonicity property. The witness_assignment is the AFTER state (slurred_speech=true) whose acuity (self_care) is strictly lower than its slurred_speech=false counterpart (emergent).
